title: The Macro Minute: Is the Fed comfortable with a stock market crash to tighten financial conditions?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Delta,
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
November 4th, 2025. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's lead off morning note. Let's
dive right in. Today's key macro
question is, is the Powell Fed
comfortable with the stock market crash
to tighten financial conditions?
Our short answer is probably not, but we
can't rule out this developing
possibility given their long and growing
history of policy errors.
In terms of analytical nuance, the 42
macro paradigm C bull market continues
to stall amid concerns of lofty
valuations being voiced by senior Wall
Street executives, which undermined
investor optimism.
Contributing to the pullback in investor
sentiment was the sell the news event
surrounding Palantir's earnings, which
saw it blow past Q3 sales expectations
and raise its revenue forecast.
Palantir stock, which is down 10% today
and still up nearly 150% year-to-date,
closed Monday at a record $207 with an
85 price-to-sales ratio, the
[clears throat] highest in the S&P 500.
When investors
when investors forcibly sell stocks that
beat and raise guidance, it speaks
volumes to how crowded the positioning
cycle is.
While the positioning cycle itself is
not a catalyst, the fact that the stock
market peaked right before the Powell
Fed signaled it was comfortable with
tightening financial conditions when it
forcibly pushed back against market
expectations for a December rate cut
last week is notable.
Per last week's FOMC statement, the Fed
is currently scheduled to end its
balance sheet runoff program on December
1st. The fact that today is only
November 4th suggests there's a lot of
time and space for big problems to
manifest, which could have spillover
effects upon broader financial markets,
especially given the positioning cycle
concerns.
To the latter point, our positioning
model now indicates there is high risk
of a crash in risk assets over the
medium to long term, as evidenced by the
quantity of stable indicators in the
model reaching their respectable market
peak thresholds.
Perhaps most concerning is the fact that
these indicators are currently as
lopsidedly bullish as they have ever
been on a median basis at historical
bull market peaks with the exception of
the July 1998 peak.
The Powell Fed clearly does not
understand the role the institution must
play in this war turning or to address
the K-shaped economic crisis, KEC, and
markets may punish them and us for that
over the next few weeks.
We currently anticipate the price will
be manageable within the context of our
paradigm C theme, but we could be wrong
about that. Stay tuned.
As always, members of our global
investor community can trust that our
institutional grade risk management
overlays, KISS and Doctor Mo, will help
our portfolios navigate these emergent
risks better than investors attempting
to manage risk without systematic rules
or relying on fundamental research
alone.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard. As
always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
Just a Thought. So, it's a it's a pretty
long one, so let me just click into it
so we can read the whole question.
It says, Just a thought, I'm part of a
private investing club that occasionally
discusses different research and
investing platforms, and I get the sense
that many people don't really get 42
macro. It came up again the other day
and the same question resurfaced,
basically in four different forms, and I
couldn't help but think that a simple
one or two page PDF would clear up clear
this up once and for all. So, the
question is, how should you handle
buying and selling when KISS and Doctor
Mo KISS or Doctor Mo changes? It says,
Now, I know this is covered in the FAQ,
but people tend to ask it in four
specific contexts. Number one, when
you're still accumulating. Number two,
when you're withdrawing. Number three,
when it's a taxable account. And number
four, when it's tax deferred or tax
exempt account. I think a short PDF
could walk through this in a structured
way. Maybe start with a simple top-down
and bottom-up presentation overlay and
then reference the backtest results. And
finally, walk through those four
scenarios with straightforward guidance.
I'm not saying it would drive a ton of
new subscribers, but it would
really help people better understand how
to apply the platform's framework in
practice. Maybe it's a small idea, but
it could make a difference.
So, I'll start by saying that if you are
evaluating a risk management overlay
through the lens of your very specific,
you know, unique tax situation in terms
of the accounts that you have your
assets
in in terms of the rules of the accounts
that you have your assets in, or in
terms of your own specific tax
you know, tax situation, I think you're
doing yourself and your family a
disservice. You know, full stop.
The whole purpose of using institutional
grade risk management overlays to guide
your asset allocation and portfolio
construction is to stay on the right
side of market risk. If you prioritize
whatever your
you know, your tax considerations are
over staying on the right side of market
risk, that's a choice you're making, but
be aware that you you know, you can't
serve two masters here. You have to
choose between staying on the right side
of market risk
or prioritizing your unique tax
situation that is unique to you, the
single person that you are in the
context of the millions if not billions
of investors around the world.
We cannot design a risk management
overlay that satisfies everyone's very
unique, highly specific tax situation.
So, what we did instead is optimize it
for staying on the right side of market
risk. And so, some of you may be able to
implement all the signals with no
penalty in your tax accounts or
whatever, or some of you may have
penalties. I don't care. That's not the
point. The point is to stay on the right
side of market risk. Again, you can
optimize for one or the other, but you
cannot optimize for both. So, you, the
investor, has to make a choice. What are
you optimizing for?
Explosive gains in bull markets and
chopping off the left tail of the
distribution when you're in a bear
market, or are you maximizing to have
terrible returns but really good tax
consequences?
Again, you cannot serve two masters. You
can either have great returns and great
risk management in bear markets, or you
can have average to below average
returns with fantastic results on from a
tax perspective. Which one do you want?
You have to make that choice. You cannot
serve two masters. And so,
wrapping up, I'll just read what one of
our other customers said.

again.

here.
This is how I modify KISS for my
personal accounts, not advice, in in the
following sell quickly but
but slowly strategy. So, number one, so
I'm going to go back and forth between
the the the four scenarios. Number one,
when you're still accumulating, he
advised that this person this investment
advisor advises his clients to follow
KISS as is. If that's too much work, I
said and forget 42 macro 60/30/10.
Number two, when you're withdrawing.
Keep one year's worth of withdrawals in
cash, 5 to 10%, and follow KISS with the
rest. I refill the cash bucket during
the year when the sell signals
materialize.
Number three, when it's a taxable
account.
Follow KISS as is. At the highest
effective federal and state tax rate,
which is 50.3 federal plus California,
KISS would have to grow at slightly over
9% per year to offset a tax free fund
paying 4.5%.
From my calculation, KISS has returned
over 12% per year since 2018. I don't
let the tax tail wag the dog 2.5x growth
dog. So, and just real quick, it's
actually 17%. I don't know if that's a
typo, but the KISS
rolling out a simple backtest that we
perform and maintain for KISS has a CAGR
of plus 17% since January 1st, 2018. So,
just be aware of that. And then finally,
when it's a tax deferred or tax exempt
account, follow KISS signals as is. So,
uh
in
four in 3.5 or really four of these
scenarios, you should be following KISS
as is. And then the one scenario
scenario where there's a real
you know, kind of retirement withdrawal
consideration, this person keeps one
year's worth of withdrawals in cash, and
then he uses the rest to follow KISS as
is. So, in conclusion, as this person
writes,
So, as Darius tells us every week,
retail investors should just follow
KISS. Again,
I have not come across I mean, I've been
doing this for almost two decades now.
I've not come across a system
with such a positively skewed return
distribution.
The Calmar ratio of KISS is 1.4. Calmar
ratio is your compound annual growth
rate
divided by your max drawdown on the
strategy.
The Calmar ratio of 60/40 is 0.4. And if
you wanted to run 60/30/10 stocks gold
Bitcoin with no
risk management overlay, so you're just
always long and you're suffering the
drawdowns associated with that strategy,
the Calmar ratio is 0.8. So, KISS,
if you're implementing KISS, you have a
basically a 300% upside capture ratio
relative to 60/40 with only a 60%
downside capture ratio. If you're
implementing KISS relative to 60/30/10
stocks gold Bitcoin, you have a roughly
90% upside capture ratio with a 50%
downside capture ratio. It's about as
positively skewed return distribution as
anything you're ever going to touch,
[clears throat] see, taste, or feel as a
retail investor. And so, this goes back
to my original point. Don't fix what
isn't broken.
Stop worrying about taxes. Stop serving
the tax master.
You know, if you're you're you're you're
you're basically if you're in
California,
you're basically you need to the only
thing you need to return stream you need
to to offset a a tax
a taxable account in California is 4.5%.
KIS's returns, according to that rolling
out of sample backtest, almost four
times that.
So, should you be concerned about paying
taxes? Absolutely not. You're you're
trouncing any sort of tax benefit you
get from
hearing to a worse strategy that does
not manage risk. And oh, by the way, the
underlying question in all this is
should I manage risk
or should I pay taxes? Or should I not
pay taxes?
And I'm telling you right now, if you're
at or near retirement,
and you choose to serve the tax master
instead of the risk management master,
you have a great chance of losing 20 to
30, upwards of 50% of all the money you
spent your life accumulating,
because you're so hyper-focused on taxes
that you fail to manage risk. And so, to
me, I think it's you have to make a
choice. You know, no no strategy is
perfect. No strategy is going to give
you such a positively skewed return
distribution without managing risk. And
so, obviously you have to choose between
managing risk or serving the tax master.
If you serve the tax master, just know
that you might blow up your account
trying to avoid taxes because you're not
managing risk like KIS does. So, wrap it
up there. Jerry Stiel here for
presenting our MacroMonitor for Tuesday,
November 4th, 2025. Best of luck out
there today. We'll catch back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
